subject: Project Management
title: The Manufacturing Metaphor
description: A micro-lesson that makes The Manufacturing Metaphor usable.

hook.text: Waterfall borrowed its logic from manufacturing: plan everything first, then build it once.

buildup.text: In factories, changing a production line mid-run is catastrophically expensive. Planning up front saves money.

discovery.text: Early software engineers assumed code was like hardware — expensive to change after production started.

twist.text: Software is fundamentally different from hardware. Code can be changed cheaply. The metaphor was wrong.

climax.text: The manufacturing metaphor shaped decades of software practice. Its shadow still haunts the industry.

punchline.text: Software isn't a factory. Treating it like one was the original sin.

quiz.question: Why was the manufacturing metaphor misleading for software?
options: Software can be changed cheaply, unlike factory production lines
Software is always expensive to change
